prerequisites: Recent marathon completed within the past 2-4 weeks
At least one week of easy running already completed post-marathon
No residual injuries or lingering soreness from the marathon
Familiar with VDOT or pace-based training
Experience with threshold and interval training

philosophy.approach: Three-phase 10K block for a runner transitioning from marathon to a faster distance, designed to bridge into a subsequent marathon training block. Reintroduction → threshold and VO2max development → race-specific sharpening.
key_features: Two quality sessions per week (Tuesday and Thursday by default)
Saturday long run with one rest day per week
Threshold (T) and interval (I) work as primary stimuli
Race-specific 10K-pace work introduced in final 3 weeks
Strides only — no hill sprints, suitable for runners with hip/groin injury history
Mid-plan 5K race in week 3 to consolidate VDOT before sharper work begins
philosophy.description_short: 10-week 10K plan for an experienced runner transitioning from marathon to a 10K focus block. Two quality sessions per week, peak ~100 km. Threshold and VO2max development followed by race-specific sharpening. Bridges into a subsequent marathon training cycle.

good_fit: Experienced runners returning from a marathon to a 10K focus
Runners with hip, groin or hamstring injury history (no hill sprints)
Athletes bridging from one marathon cycle to the next via a shorter focus block
Runners comfortable at 80-100 km per week
not_recommended: Runners without recent marathon experience (use a standalone 10K plan)
New runners or those without VDOT/threshold training experience
Runners who cannot commit to 6 days per week

full_template.name: Post-Marathon 10K (10-Week Bespoke)
full_template.author: Custom
full_template.source: Bespoke design — not derived from a published methodology

source_reference.pacing_guidance_note: All paces are derived from VDOT. Threshold (T) work uses Daniels' tempo pace; Interval (I) work uses VDOT interval pace; race-specific work in the final phase uses goal 10K race pace. If the athlete races the 5K in week 3 and the result warrants it, update VDOT before week 4.

full_template.description: Three-phase 10-week training plan for an experienced runner transitioning from marathon training to a 10K focus block, with the design intent of bridging into a subsequent marathon training cycle. Phase 1 (weeks 1-3) reintroduces structured quality work and culminates in a 5K race. Phase 2 (weeks 4-7) develops threshold and VO2max as the primary 10K-specific stimuli. Phase 3 (weeks 8-10) introduces race-specific 10K-pace work and tapers into the goal race. Volume is moderate (peak ~100 km) and quality is concentrated in two weekly sessions to leave room for aerobic maintenance.

prerequisites: Recent marathon completed within the past 2-4 weeks
At least one week of easy running already completed post-marathon
No residual injuries or lingering soreness from the marathon
Familiar with VDOT or pace-based training
Experience with threshold and interval training
target_audience.weekly_mileage_range: Peak 100 km. Athletes can scale total volume up or down by adjusting easy-day distance; quality sessions are fixed.

phases.name: Phase 1 — Reintroduction
phases.description: Re-establish quality-session rhythm post-marathon. Threshold work introduced first, VO2max work introduced in week 2. Volume builds from 75 to 90 km. Phase culminates in a 5K race in week 3 to consolidate VDOT before harder training.

phases.name: Phase 2 — Threshold and VO2max Development
phases.description: Build the metabolic substrate for 10K racing. Threshold sessions develop sustainable pace; VO2max sessions raise the ceiling. Volume peaks at 100 km in weeks 5-6. Two threshold-supported long runs in this phase build aerobic strength alongside threshold density.

phases.name: Phase 3 — Race-Specific Sharpening and Taper
phases.description: Convert general fitness into race-specific sharpness. Race-pace 10K work introduced in week 8. Volume drops from 90 km to ~50 km in race week. Race on Sunday of week 10.

program_structure.training_week_structure: Two quality sessions per week (Q1 on Tuesday, Q2 on Thursday by default). Long run on Saturday. Rest day on Sunday. Remaining days at E (easy) pace to reach the weekly volume target.

key_principles: All paces derived from VDOT — objective, individualised intensity
Two quality sessions per week leave room for aerobic maintenance and recovery, appropriate for a post-marathon block
Threshold (T) and interval (I) are the primary 10K-specific stimuli
Race-specific work concentrated in the final 3 weeks rather than spread thinly across the plan
Long runs in a 10K block serve aerobic maintenance, not aerobic development — capped at 24 km
No hill sprints anywhere in the plan; strides used for neuromuscular stimulus instead
Designed to bridge into a subsequent marathon training cycle — the threshold and aerobic base built here directly support marathon-specific work that follows
Mid-plan 5K race in week 3 substitutes for both Q2 and the long run that week, providing a low-cost VDOT recalibration point
philosophy.workout_distribution: Two quality sessions per week. Default placement: Q1=Tuesday, Q2=Thursday. Long run Saturday, rest day Sunday. All other days are easy running.
philosophy.approach: Three-phase periodization for an experienced runner transitioning from marathon to 10K focus, with subsequent marathon training in mind.
key_features: Two quality sessions per week (Tuesday and Thursday by default)
Saturday long run with one rest day per week
Threshold (T) and interval (I) work as primary stimuli
Race-specific 10K-pace work introduced in final 3 weeks
Strides only — no hill sprints, suitable for runners with hip/groin injury history
Mid-plan 5K race in week 3 to consolidate VDOT before sharper work begins

E.name: Easy
E.description: Comfortable conversational pace. Used for all non-quality running, warm-up, and cool-down. Distance-prescribed.
E.vdot_reference: Daniels' E pace from VDOT tables

T.name: Threshold (distance-prescribed)
T.description: Comfortably hard pace, sustainable for about 60 minutes in a race. Used for cruise intervals and km-based threshold reps.
T.vdot_reference: Daniels' T pace from VDOT tables

T_time.name: Threshold (time-prescribed)
T_time.description: Same intensity as T pace, used for time-based continuous tempo runs.
T_time.vdot_reference: Daniels' T pace from VDOT tables

I.name: Interval
I.description: VO2max effort. Hard pace sustainable for 3-5 minute reps. Used for VO2max development.
I.vdot_reference: Daniels' I pace from VDOT tables

R10.name: Race 10K Pace
R10.description: Goal 10K race pace. Used for race-specific work in the final phase. Equal to current 10K-equivalent pace from VDOT, or a slightly faster goal pace if the athlete is targeting a specific time.

workout_notation.quality_sessions: Q1 and Q2 are the two quality sessions per week. Default days: Q1=Tuesday, Q2=Thursday. All other days are E pace running.
workout_notation.pace_letters: Pace zone labels: E=Easy, T=Threshold (distance), T_time=Threshold (time-based), I=Interval (VO2max), R10=Race 10K pace
workout_notation.threshold_supported_long_run: Some long runs in Phase 2 include an embedded threshold segment. The athlete runs the long run at E pace, with the specified portion at T pace. Warm-up and cool-down at E pace bookend the T-pace segment.
workout_notation.race_substitution: In week 3, the 5K race substitutes for both Q2 and the long run. The athlete races, then adds easy cool-down volume to reach the prescribed total for the day.
workout_notation.strides: Strides are 100m accelerations to about mile pace, with full recovery between. Used for neuromuscular stimulus on easy days. Not a quality session.
workout_notation.vdot_recalibration: If the 5K race in week 3 produces a result that warrants it, update VDOT before starting week 4. This recalibrates all subsequent paces.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 4 × 1km at T pace, 90s recovery jogs (warm-up + cool-down to ~10km total)
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: Easy 10-12 km at E pace with 6 × 100m strides
weekly_schedule.friday: Easy 8-10 km at E pace, or rest
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 18 km at E pace
weekly_schedule.notes: First quality session is conservative — single threshold block, modest volume. Q2 is strides only, not a true second quality session yet, easing the athlete back into structure.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 5 × 1km at T pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 8 × 400m at I pace, 400m recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.friday: Easy 8 km at E pace, or rest
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 20 km at E pace
weekly_schedule.notes: Threshold volume increases by one rep. First proper VO2max session — modest rep length (400m) to ease the system into faster work.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 20 min continuous tempo at T_time pace
weekly_schedule.saturday: 5K race, plus 5-8 km easy cool-down at E pace to bring total to ~15 km
weekly_schedule.notes: Race week. The 5K race substitutes for both Q2 (it IS the quality session) and the long run. Tuesday's session shifts from intervals to a continuous tempo to leave the legs fresher for racing. Update VDOT after the race if the result warrants it.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 5 × 1km at T pace, 60s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 5 × 800m at I pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 22 km at E pace
weekly_schedule.notes: Recovery between threshold reps shortens to 60s, building threshold density. I-pace reps lengthen to 800m.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 2 × 3km at T pace, 3 min jog between
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 6 × 800m at I pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 22 km at E pace with last 4 km at T pace
weekly_schedule.notes: First threshold-supported long run — T-pace segment at the end when the athlete is warmed up. Threshold session moves to longer continuous blocks.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 6 × 1km at T pace, 60s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 5 × 1000m at I pace, 2 min recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 24 km at E pace with 6 km at T pace embedded mid-run (km 12-18)
weekly_schedule.notes: Peak threshold density. I-pace reps lengthen to 1000m. Threshold-supported long run with T-pace embedded mid-run — harder version of the previous week's long run.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 4 × 1.5km at T pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 4 × 1200m at I pace, 2 min recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 22 km at E pace
weekly_schedule.notes: Small cutback in volume after peak. Quality remains demanding but with slightly less total work. Long run drops embedded threshold to allow recovery before the race-specific phase.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 5 × 1km at R10 pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 25 min continuous tempo at T_time pace
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 20 km at E pace
weekly_schedule.notes: First race-pace work. Goal 10K pace is set from the most recent VDOT (updated after the week 3 race if applicable). Threshold session shifts to continuous tempo to consolidate threshold fitness.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1: 3 × 2km at R10 pace, 2 min recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Q2: 6 × 400m at I pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.saturday: Long run, 16-18 km at E pace
weekly_schedule.notes: Volume drops noticeably. Tuesday session is the key sharpener — longer race-pace segments. Thursday returns to short fast reps to keep the system primed without fatigue.

weekly_schedule.tuesday: Q1 (taper): 3 × 1km at R10 pace, 90s recovery jogs
weekly_schedule.thursday: Easy 5-6 km at E pace with 4 × 100m strides
weekly_schedule.friday: Rest, or 4-5 km very easy at E pace
weekly_schedule.saturday: Easy 4-5 km at E pace with 4 × 100m strides
weekly_schedule.notes: Standard race-week taper. One short race-pace session early in the week to keep the system primed. Strides on Thursday and Saturday to maintain neuromuscular readiness without fatigue.

race_week.guidance: Saturday before the race: easy 4-5 km with 4 strides. No quality the day before. Friday is rest or very easy. The final Q1 session on Tuesday primes the system without fatigue.

adaptation_guidelines.weekly_volume: Volumes shown are guidelines. Athletes can scale total weekly volume up or down by adjusting easy-day distance. Quality session structure is fixed; only the easy-day volume should be modified.
adaptation_guidelines.quality_day_placement: Default: Q1=Tuesday, Q2=Thursday. Q1 and Q2 can be moved to any days that maintain at least one easy day between quality sessions.
adaptation_guidelines.rest_day_placement: Default rest day is Sunday. Some athletes prefer Friday as the rest day; this is acceptable as long as one full rest day per week is maintained.
adaptation_guidelines.long_runs: Saturday long run can be moved to Sunday with rest moved to Friday, but the long run should be at least 24 hours separated from Q2.
adaptation_guidelines.no_hill_sprints: This plan deliberately omits hill sprints. Strides on flat ground are used for neuromuscular stimulus throughout. This makes the plan suitable for athletes with hip, groin, or hamstring injury history. Athletes who want hill sprints should choose a different template.
adaptation_guidelines.race_in_week_3: If the athlete cannot find a 5K race in week 3, substitute a hard solo time trial of 5K at race effort, or a parkrun-style event of similar distance. The purpose is a maximum-effort 5K to consolidate VDOT.
adaptation_guidelines.vdot_recalibration: Update VDOT after the week 3 race if the result warrants it. This recalibrates all subsequent paces, including the race-specific R10 work in Phase 3.
adaptation_guidelines.missed_quality_session: If a quality session is missed, do not double up. Skip it, do an easy run instead, and resume the schedule the following week. Missing one quality session in this plan is low-stakes; missing two in the same week suggests volume or intensity needs reduction.
adaptation_guidelines.bridging_to_marathon_training: This plan is designed to bridge into a subsequent marathon training cycle. After the race in week 10, take a transition week (50-60 km easy, no quality) before starting marathon-specific training.

E.description: Easy/recovery pace. Conversational. Used for all non-quality running, warm-up, and cool-down.

T.description: Threshold pace, distance-prescribed. Comfortably hard, ~60 min race effort. Used for cruise intervals and km-based threshold reps.

T_time.description: Threshold pace, time-prescribed. Same intensity as T, used for time-based continuous tempo runs.

I.description: Interval pace. VO2max effort, 3-5 min reps. Used for VO2max development.

R10.description: Goal 10K race pace. Used for race-specific work in the final phase. Equal to current 10K-equivalent pace from VDOT, or a slightly faster goal pace if the athlete is targeting a specific time.
